summary: German composer (1770–1827) who spanned and defined the transition from the Classical to Romantic era. Best known for his nine symphonies (especially the 5th, 6th 'Pastoral,' and 9th 'Choral'), five piano concertos (the 5th is the 'Emperor'), thirty-two piano sonatas (including 'Moonlight,' 'Pathetique,' 'Appassionata,' 'Hammerklavier'), the Violin Concerto, the opera Fidelio, the War Requiem, string quartets (including the Grosse Fuge and the 'Razumovsky' quartets), and a Missa solemnis. Wrote the song cycle An die ferne Geliebte. Famous for composing while deaf. The Beethoven Frieze by Gustav Klimt is in the Vienna Secession Building.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: German composer (1770–1827), born in Bonn, worked in Vienna. Studied with Haydn among others. Composed while progressively losing hearing; wrote his 9th Symphony while deaf. Famous for tearing up his third symphony's dedication page to Napoleon. Originally dedicated the Kreutzer Sonata to George Bridgetower (a biracial British violinist who premiered it), then re-dedicated it to Rodolphe Kreutzer after Bridgetower insulted a friend. His Opus 1 consists of three piano trios dedicated to Prince Lichnowsky. He was an early adopter of the metronome (built by Johann Nepomuk Maelzel), leading to confusingly high tempo markings. Analysis of his hair revealed elevated lead levels (likely from lead acetate in cheap wine) and Hepatitis B infection. The Beethoven Frieze by Gustav Klimt (covering three walls in the Vienna Secession Building) was inspired by his music; its central section features the giant Typhoeus and the Gorgon sisters.

clues.clue: Tore up his Symphony No. 3's dedication page to Napoleon; the symphony had been originally dedicated to him

works.name: Symphony No. 3 (Eroica)
works.description: Symphony No. 3 in E-flat major, Op. 55. Often cited as the beginning of Beethoven's middle period. Originally dedicated to Napoleon; the dedication page was torn up. Opens with two isolated E-flat major forte chords; cellos then play eight notes outlining an E-flat major triad. Violins enter in bar 7 with syncopated G's floating above a sustained C-sharp resolving to D. The second movement is an unusually long funeral march in C minor. The finale is a set of variations on a theme from Beethoven's The Creatures of Prometheus. At rehearsal, Ferdinand Ries mistakenly berated a horn player for starting the recapitulation early. Tan Dun wrote an 'Internet Symphony' inspired by it using hubcaps and brake drums. Donald Tovey describes a 'vision of dry bones' in the finale. Wikipedia

works.name: Symphony No. 5 in C minor
works.description: Symphony No. 5 in C minor, Op. 67. First movement (Allegro con brio) opens with the famous four-note 'fate' motif: 'G, G, G, long E-flat.' The first oboe plays an unexpected adagio cadenza in the recapitulation at a half cadence ending on G. C-minor scherzo includes a Mannheim rocket in low strings (pianissimo); a reprise of the scherzo interrupts the joyous C-major finale (E.M. Forster described this as a return of 'the goblins'). The first movement motif inspired Tchaikovsky's 4th Symphony. Winds and strings trade pianissimo B-diminished-seventh chords just before the retransition. Peter Schickele and Gifford Nielsen commentated over the first movement as if it were a football game in 'New Horizons in Music Appreciation.' Wikipedia

works.name: Symphony No. 6 (Pastoral)
works.description: Symphony No. 6 in F major, Op. 68, nicknamed 'Pastoral.' Five movements, with programmatic titles. A flute, oboe, and two clarinets imitate bird calls in the 'Scene by the brook.' The fourth movement depicts a storm: cellos and basses sustain a low tremolo; the piccolo and timpani appear for the first time in the work. The storm gives way to a shepherd's Thanksgiving hymn. Richard Wagner called Symphony No. 7 'the apotheosis of dance' (not No. 6). Wikipedia

Beethoven's prior symphony, his sixth, has this nickname due to its evocations of the countryside.

works.name: Symphony No. 7 in A major
works.description: Symphony No. 7 in A major, Op. 92. Richard Wagner called it 'the apotheosis of dance.' Second movement (Allegretto, but the slowest movement) features a famous long-short-short-long-long ostinato rhythm in A minor. Borrows from Beethoven's 12 Irish Songs. First movement: repeated E's from the flute transition the slow Poco Sostenuto introduction to a 6/8 Vivace dance. Fourth movement: basses play constant chromatic quarter notes in a chaotic finale; winds and strings trade an eighth note + six sixteenth note theme borrowed from 12 Irish Songs. Robert Schumann wrote variations on the Allegretto's main theme. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Richard Wagner called it 'the apotheosis of dance'; second movement (Allegretto) in A minor has a long-short-short-long-long ostinato

works.name: Symphony No. 9 (Choral)
works.description: Symphony No. 9 in D minor, Op. 125 ('Choral'). Beethoven was deaf at the premiere and had to be turned to face the audience's applause. Finale features the 'Ode to Joy' theme adapted from Friedrich Schiller's poem praising the 'daughter of Elysium.' The finale opens with a 'terror fanfare' followed by a cello/bass recitative that 'tries out' and rejects previous themes. A 100-bar section imitates janissary bands (Turkish March), with prominent piccolo, triangle, and tenor solo. Third movement modulates up a fifth into 12/8 after a French horn solo. Opening: winds enter one-by-one as violins play descending fifths and fourths. Influenced Bruckner's unfinished symphony in D minor. Premiered alongside the Missa solemnis. The Choral Fantasy (Op. 80) is a precursor. Wikipedia

works.name: Piano Sonatas
works.description: Beethoven wrote 32 piano sonatas. Key sonatas: Moonlight (No. 14, C-sharp minor, Op. 27 No. 2) — first movement has constant C-sharp minor triplet arpeggios; dedicated to student Julie Guicciardi; instruction to keep pedal down constantly; nicknamed by Ludwig Rellstab. Pathetique (No. 8, C minor, Op. 13) — slow introduction marked Grave; the development is interrupted by a return of the introduction; celebrated Adagio cantabile slow movement in A-flat inspired Elgar's 'Nimrod.' Appassionata (No. 23, F minor, Op. 57) — begins with arpeggios on the tonic and Neapolitan (G-flat major) chord. Hammerklavier (No. 29, B-flat major, Op. 106) — titled for the German name of the piano; has a three-voice Allegro risoluto fugue finale. Final sonata (No. 32, C minor, Op. 111) — finale is an arietta with variations in 9/16 time.

examples: This Beethoven piece begins with a Mannheim rocket that ranges from middle C to A-flat 5 before resolving down to F. That phrase is then repeated over a dominant chord, leading Schoenberg to use this piece's opening as an example of a musical sentence.

works.name: String Quartets
works.description: Beethoven wrote 16 string quartets; the Grosse Fuge was originally the finale of No. 13 (B-flat major, Op. 130). Three quartets dedicated to Count Razumovsky. The final string quartet (No. 16, F major, Op. 135) contains a movement titled 'The Difficult Decision' with the inscribed question 'Must it be?' answered 'It must be!' His string quartet in F major (Op. 59 No. 1) begins with a 24-bar overture of unison G's. String Quartet No. 7 (Op. 59 No. 1) begins with the notes G-sharp, B-sharp, C-sharp, A. One late quartet (String Quartet in E-flat major, Op. 127) was played on Schubert's deathbed. The Beethoven Quartet (Moscow ensemble) premiered 13 of Shostakovich's 15 quartets.

clues.clue: The Grosse Fuge was originally the finale of the String Quartet No. 13 (B-flat major, Op. 130); then published independently
examples: one of these works, called 'contemporary forever' by Igor Stravinsky, was originally the finale of the 13th of these works
Borodin's First String Quartet is based on material from this late Beethoven chamber piece, from which the Grosse Fuge was extracted and independently published.

clues.clue: Final string quartet (No. 16, F major, Op. 135): final movement titled 'The Difficult Decision'; inscribed 'Must it be? It must be!'
examples: The last movement of this chamber work contains the motifs... titled 'The Difficult Decision.' This work's composer wrote the question 'Must it be?' in its manuscript, to which he himself responded 'It must be!'

examples: Late in life, he wrote a string quartet centered around a 'holy song of thanksgiving' in F Lydian.
One of these works with seven movements was played on Franz Schubert's deathbed.

works.name: Violin Concerto and Piano Concertos
works.description: Violin Concerto (D major, Op. 61): opens with five timpani taps; dedicated to Franz Clement ('for Clement, with clemency'); soloist plays a 'hunting horn' theme on the G-string in the third movement rondo; Larghetto second movement in G major; premiered with soloist Franz Clement; characterized as 'the greatest, most uncompromising' by Joseph Joachim; Beethoven also arranged it as a Piano Concerto. Emperor Concerto (Piano Concerto No. 5, E-flat major): opens with a tutti E-flat chord followed by ascending arpeggios by the soloist; ends with an E-flat major rondo; finale is attacca from the second movement. Piano Concerto No. 4 (G major): opens with soft chordal theme repeated by strings in B major (mediant); second movement has staccato E-minor strings evoking Orpheus's pleas to the Furies (per A. B. Marx).

works.name: Other Works
works.description: Other major works: Fidelio (only opera); Missa solemnis in D major (dedicated to Archduke Rudolf; premiered with the 9th Symphony); Choral Fantasy Op. 80 (a precursor to the 9th — repeats 'Divine grace is bestowed upon Man'; famously fell apart at premiere); Wellington's Victory (for which he worked with Maelzel on the metronome); Piano Trios including 'Archduke' (No. 11, for Archduke Rudolph) and 'Ghost' (Carl Czerny said its Largo evoked Hamlet's father); Septet in E-flat (for clarinet, bassoon, horn, and strings); 'Les Adieux' Sonata (Op. 81a, opens with the German translation of its nickname); Turkish March (Op. 76 in B-flat major, used for the play The Ruins of Athens).

examples: A symphony named for this adjective [choral] premiered in Vienna alongside its composer's Missa Solemnis.

comprehensive_summary:
Ludwig van Beethoven (1770–1827) was a German composer who spanned the transition from the Classical to Romantic era, working primarily in Vienna. He composed nine symphonies, five piano concertos, thirty-two piano sonatas, a violin concerto, seventeen string quartets, one opera (Fidelio), and a Missa solemnis, among many other works. He famously composed while progressively losing his hearing, writing his Ninth Symphony while completely deaf — he had to be turned to face the audience's applause at the premiere.

His Third Symphony ('Eroica,' E-flat major, Op. 55) is often cited as the beginning of his middle period. Originally dedicated to Napoleon, the dedication was furiously torn up. It opens with two isolated E-flat forte chords; in the seventh bar, violins enter with syncopated G's above a sustained C-sharp resolving to D. The second movement is an unusually long C-minor funeral march, and the finale is a set of variations on a theme from his ballet The Creatures of Prometheus. Symphony No. 5 (C minor, Op. 67) opens with the famous four-note 'fate knocks at the door' motif (G, G, G, long E-flat). At the recapitulation, the first oboe plays an unexpected adagio cadenza on a half cadence. The C-minor scherzo opens with a pianissimo Mannheim rocket; a quiet pizzicato reprise of the scherzo interrupts the joyous C-major finale — E.M. Forster described this as a return of 'the goblins.' The motif inspired Tchaikovsky's Fourth Symphony. Symphony No. 6 ('Pastoral,' F major, Op. 68) has five programmatic movements, including bird calls from flute, oboe, and clarinets in the 'Scene by the brook,' and a storm movement (cellos and basses sustain a low tremolo; piccolo and timpani appear for the first time) giving way to a Thanksgiving hymn. Symphony No. 7 (A major, Op. 92) was called 'the apotheosis of dance' by Wagner; its second movement (marked Allegretto but the symphony's slowest) features a famous long-short-short-long-long ostinato in A minor; it borrows from Beethoven's 12 Irish Songs. Symphony No. 9 (D minor, Op. 125, 'Choral') has a finale featuring the 'Ode to Joy' from Schiller's poem. The finale opens with a 'terror fanfare' followed by a cello/bass recitative that rejects previous themes; a 100-bar Turkish March section imitates janissary bands with piccolo, triangle, and a tenor solo.

Key piano sonatas: the Moonlight Sonata (No. 14, C-sharp minor, Op. 27 No. 2) opens with constant triplet arpeggios; the instruction requires the pedal to be held down constantly; dedicated to student Julie Guicciardi; nicknamed by Ludwig Rellstab. The Pathetique Sonata (No. 8, C minor, Op. 13) features a slow Grave introduction interrupted again in the development section; its Adagio cantabile in A-flat inspired Elgar's 'Nimrod.' The Appassionata (No. 23, F minor) begins with arpeggios on the tonic and G-flat Neapolitan chord. The Hammerklavier (No. 29, B-flat major) is titled for the German name of the piano and ends with a three-voice Allegro risoluto fugue. His final sonata (No. 32, C minor) ends with an arietta with variations in 9/16 time.

The Violin Concerto (D major, Op. 61) opens with five timpani taps and was dedicated to Franz Clement; characterized as 'the greatest, most uncompromising' by Joachim. The Emperor Concerto (Piano Concerto No. 5, E-flat major) opens with a tutti chord immediately followed by arpeggios from the soloist. His string quartets include the Grosse Fuge (originally the finale of String Quartet No. 13, Op. 130) and three Razumovsky quartets; the final string quartet (No. 16, F major, Op. 135) contains 'The Difficult Decision' movement with the inscription 'Must it be? It must be!'

Beethoven originally dedicated the Kreutzer Sonata to the biracial British violinist George Bridgetower, who premiered it, then re-dedicated it to Rodolphe Kreutzer after a quarrel. His Opus 1 consists of three piano trios for Prince Lichnowsky; the Archduke Trio was for Archduke Rudolf. He wrote An die ferne Geliebte (often called the first song cycle, six poems by Alois Isidor Jetteles), quoted in Schumann's Fantasy in C. The Beethoven Frieze by Gustav Klimt covers three walls of the Vienna Secession Building. His Missa solemnis in D major was dedicated to Archduke Rudolf and premiered at the same concert as the Ninth Symphony. Analysis of his hair revealed elevated lead levels (likely from lead acetate in cheap wine) and Hepatitis B.

cards.front: Composer: German; composed his 9th Symphony while completely deaf; famous for tearing up Symphony No. 3's dedication page to Napoleon
Composer: wrote An die ferne Geliebte (often called the first song cycle), setting six poems by Alois Isidor Jetteles; quoted in Schumann's Fantasy in C
Composer: originally dedicated the Kreutzer Sonata to George Bridgetower (who premiered it); re-dedicated it to Rodolphe Kreutzer after Bridgetower insulted a friend
Composer: the Beethoven Frieze by Gustav Klimt covers three walls of the Vienna Secession Building; its central section features the giant Typhoeus and the Gorgons
Symphony: E-flat major; bears a retracted dedication to Napoleon; second movement is an unusually long funeral march; finale is a set of variations on a theme from The Creatures of Prometheus
Symphony: opens with two isolated E-flat forte chords; violins enter in bar 7 with syncopated G's above a sustained C-sharp resolving to D
Symphony: opens with 'G, G, G, long E-flat' — the 'fate knocks at the door' motif; first oboe plays an unexpected adagio cadenza in the recapitulation
Symphony: C-minor scherzo (pizzicato) quietly reprises in the middle of the C-major finale; E. M. Forster described this as a return of 'the goblins'
Symphony: 'Pastoral'; five programmatic movements; bird calls from flute, oboe, and clarinets in 'Scene by the brook'; storm with low tremolo gives way to a Thanksgiving hymn
Symphony: Wagner called it 'the apotheosis of dance'; second movement (Allegretto, but slowest) has a long-short-short-long-long ostinato in A minor
Symphony: 'Choral'; finale opens with a 'terror fanfare,' then cello/bass recitative rejects previous themes; Turkish March section imitates janissary bands with piccolo, triangle, and tenor
Sonata: C-sharp minor; first movement has constant triplet arpeggios; instruction to hold pedal down constantly; dedicated to student Julie Guicciardi; nicknamed by Ludwig Rellstab
Sonata: C minor; slow Grave introduction returns in the development; Adagio cantabile in A-flat inspired Elgar's 'Nimrod'
Sonata: F minor; begins with arpeggios on the tonic chord and the Neapolitan (G-flat major) chord
Sonata: B-flat major; named for the German word for the piano; ends with a three-voice Allegro risoluto fugue
Concerto: Violin Concerto in D major; opens with five timpani taps; dedicated to Franz Clement ('with clemency, for Clement'); called 'greatest, most uncompromising' by Joseph Joachim
Concerto: 'Emperor' (No. 5, E-flat major); opens with a tutti E-flat chord followed by ascending arpeggios from the soloist; finale is attacca from the second movement
Work: originally the finale of String Quartet No. 13 (B-flat major, Op. 130); published independently; called 'contemporary forever' by Stravinsky
String Quartet: No. 16, F major; final movement 'The Difficult Decision' inscribed with 'Must it be? It must be!'
